vocab.meaning: dead — irregular past participle of morir

grammar.title: ‘En otro tiempo’ + imperfect
grammar.detail: ‘En otro tiempo ustedes estaban muertos…’ Imperfect tense (estaban) describes a continuing state in the past — the natural choice when sketching the way things ‘used to be’.

grammar.title: estar + adjective for state
grammar.detail: estaban muertos = ‘they were (in the state of) being dead’. Spanish prefers estar over ser when describing a condition that, in principle, could change.

expression.phrase: muertos en sus transgresiones y pecados
expression.note: Paul uses ‘dead’ as a spiritual category — not biologically dead, but cut off from God’s life. The phrase sets up the dramatic contrast in v.4 (‘But God…’).

vocab.meaning: to walk, to live one’s life (biblical idiom)

grammar.title: Imperfect ‘andaban / se conducían’
grammar.detail: Both verbs are imperfect — habitual past actions. The whole verse paints a picture of how the readers used to live, with no fixed endpoint.

grammar.title: Reflexive ‘se conducían’
grammar.detail: conducirse = to conduct oneself, behave. The reflexive ‘se’ makes the verb intransitive.

expression.note: ‘to walk according to…’ — a Hebrew-flavoured metaphor: the way you walk = the way you live. Used constantly in Paul.

grammar.title: ‘por naturaleza’
grammar.detail: ‘by nature’ — fixed prepositional phrase, no article. ‘por’ marks the cause/means.

grammar.title: ser + objeto de
grammar.detail: ‘éramos objeto de la ira de Dios’ — to be the object/target of something. Imperfect ‘éramos’ keeps the description in the same habitual past as the rest of vv.1–3.

expression.phrase: objeto de la ira de Dios
expression.note: Paraphrases the Hebrew idiom ‘children of wrath’ (Eph 2:3 KJV/ESV). NVI smooths it into modern Spanish — ‘the targets of God’s wrath’.
